revision.text:
{{short description|Bodhisattva and female Wisdom King}} {{Chinese | title = Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī | pic = Kujaku Myoo edit.jpg | picsize = | piccap = Painting of Mahāmāyūrī, pigment on silk. Japan, [[Heian Period]], 12th century CE | san = {{IAST|Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī}} | l = Peacock Wisdom Queen | t = 孔雀明王 | s = 孔雀明王 | p = Kǒngquè Míngwáng | w = | mi = | vie = Khổng Tước Minh Vương | kanji = 孔雀明王 | romaji = Kujaku Myōō | hangul = 공작명왕 | hanja = | rr = Gongjak Myeongwang }} '''Mahamayuri''' ({{langx|sa|महामायूरी}} {{IAST|''Mahāmāyūrī''}} ("great peacock"), {{zh|孔雀明王}} ''Kǒngquè Míngwáng'', {{langx|vi|Khổng Tước Minh Vương}}, {{langx|ja|孔雀明王|''Kujaku Myōō''}}, {{langx|ko|공작명왕}} ''Gongjak Myeongwang''), or '''Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī''' is a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] and female [[Wisdom King]] in [[Mahayana]] and [[Vajrayana]] Buddhism. In the latter tradition, Mahamayuri is a popular practice in both the Chinese and Japanese forms of Vajrayana. She is also the name of one of the five protective goddesses in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003">{{cite book|author1=John C. Huntington|author2=Dina Bangdel|title=The Circle of Bliss: Buddhist Meditational Art|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l3KmWbcq5foC |year=2003|publisher=Serindia Publications |isbn=978-1-932476-01-9|pages=24–25}}</ref><ref name="Dalal2010p218">{{cite book|author=Roshen Dalal|title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&pg=PA218 |year=2010|publisher=Penguin |isbn=978-0-14-341517-6|page=218}}</ref><ref name="Shaw2006p233">{{cite book|author=Miranda Eberle Shaw|title=Buddhist Goddesses of India|url=https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw |url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-12758-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw/page/233 233], 339–341, 465 note 77}}</ref>
In Chinese Buddhism and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]], it is believed that the Great Peacock King is an incarnation of either [[Vairocana Buddha]] or [[Shakyamuni Buddha]]. Its main merits include the elimination of poison and disease, the protection of the country, the dispelling of disasters, and the ability to pray for both rain and cessation of rain.<ref>'''佛母五大明王'''（五位女性明王）指：大随求菩萨 大隨求明王、大千摧碎明王、隨持密咒明王、大寒林明王、大孔雀明王五位佛母；也就相對：《大隨求陀羅尼經》、《守護大千國土大明王陀羅尼經》、《大威德神呪經》、《尸多林經》、《佛母大孔雀明王經》五部經典。</ref>

Known as the 'Queen of the secret sciences' and the 'Godmother of Buddha',<ref>{{cite web|title=(孔雀明王咒) 的經典|url=http://paper.udn.com/udnpaper/POL0005/136113/web/|access-date=17 November 2017|work=paper.udn.com|language=zh}}</ref> Mahamayuri is believed to have the power to protect devotees from poisoning, either physical or spiritual. In Buddhism, her demeanor is in contrast to the wrathful attitudes of male personifications of the [[Wisdom King]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Art Institute of Chicago|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PfLzXKeVKzEC&pg=RA1-PA36|title=Notable Acquisitions at the Art Institute of Chicago|publisher=University of Illinois Press|year=2004|isbn=978-0-86559-209-4|page=36}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=True Buddha Dharma-character Treasury - Mahamayuri|url=http://tbsn.org/english2/news.php?id=469&classid=1|access-date=17 November 2017|work=tbsn.org|language=en}}</ref>

Unlike her four companions of the pañcarakṣā she appears to have had a fairly well-developed cult in India.<ref name="myoo">{{cite web |title=JAANUS / Kujaku Myouou 孔雀明王 |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kujakumyouou.htm |website=www.aisf.or.jp}}</ref>

She is often seated on a lotus flower or a peacock throne. The right hand holds a lotus flower, which represents the purity of his wisdom. The second right hand holds the fruit of all causes, which represents his ability to fulfill all wishes. The left hand holds a wish-fulfilling fruit, which represents his ability to grant all desires. The second left hand holds three or five peacock tails, which represent his power to dispel poison and disease. In the Garbhadhātu Mandala, Mahamayuri is enshrined in the sixth position of the southernmost corner of the {{ill|Susiddhivīja|ja|蘇悉地院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susiddhivīja|qq=}}.<ref name="legend"/>
She is often portrayed riding a peacock and commonly sporting four arms. Although the items she holds vary among traditions, common items include a [[:en:Citron|Citron]], [[:en:bael fruit|bael fruit]], lotus flower, and a peacock tail feather. With the moon as a background, wear peaceful ornaments and garments. Seated in the half (vajrasana) posture.<ref name=v/>
==Legends== [[File:孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō.jpg|thumb|孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō Image of Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī from the Taishō Shinshū Daizōkyō]] Legend holds that in primordial times, the original [[Phoenix (mythology)|Phoenix]] ([[:en:Fenghuang|Fenghuang]]), the leader of flying beings, gave birth to the peacock Mahamayuri and to the eagle named the [[:en:Dapeng Jinchi Mingwang|Golden-Winged Great Peng]]. The peacock once consumed the Buddha in one of his previous lives, who managed to escape by cutting through her stomach. At that time, the peacock preyed on humans, and the Buddha intended to kill it. However, the deities intervened and urged him to stop. In exchange for a promise to renounce its habit of preying on humans, the Buddha elevated the peacock to the status of his godmother, while the eagle became his uncle and was granted a high position in heaven.<ref>{{cite news |title=西遊最強姐弟，為何最後卻反目成仇，只因分別效忠兩位佛祖 |url=https://www.xuehua.us/2019/03/10/西游最强姐弟，为何最后却反目成仇，只因分别效/zh-tw/ |work=雪花新聞 |date=9 March 2019|language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊記中除了金翅大鵬，如來的親戚都是誰？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/CrkZgB |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=4 September 2017 |language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=9789866936333 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B+%E8%80%81%E9%B9%B0 |language=zh-TW}}</ref>
In the ''[[Mahamayuri Tantra]]'', it is stated that Mahamayuri assumes various forms to assist the Buddha, including taking on the human form of [[Queen Maya]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Mahamayuri Tantra |url=https://www.scribd.com/document/404209978/Mahamayuri-pdf |language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Kong Xuan|Kong Xuan]], a peacock spirit in the Ming Dynasty novel ''[[:en:Fengshen Yanyi|Fengshen Yanyi]]'', is based on the figure of Mahamayuri. Kong Xuan appears as a powerful general under [[:en:King Zhou of Shang|King Zhou of Shang]].<ref>{{cite book |title=中国精怪文化 [Chinese Ghost Culture] |date=1997 |publisher=上海人民出版社 |isbn=978-7-208-02454-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SSZhAAAAIAAJ&q=%E5%AD%94%E5%AE%A3+%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Journey to the West''=== The 16th-century Chinese novel ''[[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]]'' includes a story about how the king of the Kingdom of Zhuzi had a hobby of [[:en:Hunting|Hunting]] when he was a young prince. One day, he accidentally killed two followers of Mahamayuri. Later on, Mahamayuri said that to pay for this, the prince would suffer a lovesickness for 3 years. At the time, the bodhisattva [[Kannon|Guanyin]]'s steed [[:en:Denglong (mythology)|Golden Haired Hou]] went across the scene and managed to hear this. Years later, when the prince had grown up and became the king of the kingdom, the Golden Haired Hou took advantage of the child who guards him sleeping, escaped to the mortal realm, became the demon king Sai Tai Sui, and kidnapped the Lady of Jinsheng Palace (金聖宮娘娘), one of the king's favorite consorts, and forced her to marry him. Without the lady, the king got sick for three years.<ref>{{cite news |title=赛太岁很听话？观音菩萨"打诳语"有水平_手机搜狐网 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/107874164_109477/?pvid=000115_3w_a |work=Sohu|language=my|date=27 July 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊朱紫國王射傷了孔雀明王的孩子，為何是觀音坐騎出面復仇？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/Wjo7g5 |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=8 August 2017 |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra''=== According to the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'', during the time of the Buddha, there was a recently ordained monk named Sāḍdhi. Despite his brief time as a monk, he had already received the precepts and was diligently studying the Vinaya rules. While engaged in gathering firewood and preparing a bath for fellow monks, Sāḍdhi encountered a perilous situation when a large black snake emerged from a hole in a decaying tree and bit him on his right big toe. The venom swiftly spread throughout his body, causing him to collapse unconscious on the ground, foaming at the mouth, with his eyes rolled back.<ref name="legend">{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=978-986-6936-33-3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
[[:en:Ananda|Ananda]], witnessing the distressing scene, promptly sought the Buddha's assistance. In response, the Buddha informed Ananda about the potent mantra of the Mahamayuri (Great Peacock) Buddha Mother, emphasizing its ability to counteract poisons, dispel fears, avert calamities, and safeguard and nurture all sentient beings, leading them to happiness. The Buddha proceeded to impart the Mahamayuri Vidyarajni mantra to Ananda, who hastened back to Sāḍdhi and employed the mantra for healing. Through the power of the mantra, the poison was eradicated, and Sāḍdhi regained consciousness, fully restored to health. Expressing the significance of the mantra, the Buddha instructed Ananda to propagate it among all sentient beings, offering protection from harm and the opportunity to attain happiness. The ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' is a Mahayana Buddhist scripture that imparts the mantra of the Mahamayuri Buddha Mother. This mantra is reputed for its ability to safeguard against harm, alleviate ailments, and usher in happiness. Widely embraced by Mahayana Buddhism practitioners, the mantra is frequently chanted as a means of spiritual practice.<ref name="legend"/>
The Buddha imparted the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' with the specific intention of addressing the manifold challenges faced by sentient beings. According to the sutra, both the text and the mantra possess the extraordinary power to alleviate diseases, afflictions, and fears while serving as a protective shield for all living beings. Instructing Ananda to disseminate this sutra and mantra widely, the Buddha aimed to liberate sentient beings from suffering and guide them towards the attainment of happiness.<ref name="legend"/>
Delving into the origin of the Buddha's emphasis on this mantra, the sutra recounts a past life where the Buddha existed as a golden peacock king residing on the southern slope of [[Mount Meru]]. Engaging in the daily recitation of the mantra, the king experienced perpetual peace in mind and body. However, a lapse occurred when, driven by desire, he neglected the mantra to frolic with his peacock consorts in the forest. Succumbing to intoxication and a loss of mindfulness, he inadvertently fell into a hunter's trap. Bound and facing peril, he remembered the mantra, reciting it and miraculously securing his release, with his consorts emerging unscathed.<ref name="legend"/>
Recognizing the formidable efficacy of this mantra, the Buddha discerned its capacity to mitigate grave transgressions, eradicate minor wrongdoings, and dispel all forms of suffering. Out of compassion, the Buddha shared this transformative mantra with all sentient beings, envisioning that its constant recitation would empower individuals to transcend fear, surmount suffering, perpetually attract good fortune, evade danger, and live long and contented lives.<ref name="legend"/>
== In Chinese Buddhism == In China, she is known as Kǒngquè Míngwáng (孔雀明王) and her image is enshrined in many Chinese Buddhist temples. In contemporary times, her veneration is thought to be closely related to medical care, public health, environmental protection, and social welfare.
In [[:en:China|China]], some of the temples which venerate her include the [[Luohan Temple (Chongqing)|Luohan Temple]] in [[:en:Chongqing|Chongqing]], [[Wuyou Temple]], [[:en:Baoguang Temple|Baoguang Temple]] and [[:en:Fuhu Temple|Fuhu Temple]] in [[Sichuan]], [[Shifo Temple]] in [[Shaanxi]], [http://www.jgms.net/ Jinguangming Temple] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], as well as [[Qiongzhu Temple]] in [[Yunnan]]. In [[Taiwan]], her veneration is promoted by the [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about.html Chinese Great Peacock Buddha Association](中華世界大孔雀佛協會), which has their main headquarters located at [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about01.html Kaicheng Temple]. In [[Singapore]], one temple which venerates her is [[:en:Hua Giam Si|Hua Gim Si Temple]].
==In Japanese Buddhism== In Japan she was known as the name Kujaku Myōō (孔雀明王). There is a record stating that she was worshiped during the [[Nara period]], and her image was placed at the newly constructed [[Saidai-ji]] Temple Kondo (Saidai-ji Temple Golden Hall).<ref name="myoo"/>
== Gallery == <gallery> Banner with Bodhisattva, possibly Mahamayuri.jpg| Chinesischer Maler des 11. Jahrhunderts (II) 001.jpg| Dazu 2007 665.jpg| Mahamayuri statue.jpg| LK-colombo-gangaramaya-pfau.jpg| </gallery>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{short description|Bodhisattva and female Wisdom King}} {{Chinese | title = Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī | pic = Kujaku Myoo edit.jpg | picsize = | piccap = Painting of Mahāmāyūrī, pigment on silk. Japan, [[Heian Period]], 12th century CE | san = {{IAST|Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī}} | l = Peacock Wisdom Queen | t = 孔雀明王 | s = 孔雀明王 | p = Kǒngquè Míngwáng | w = | mi = | vie = Khổng Tước Minh Vương | kanji = 孔雀明王 | romaji = Kujaku Myōō | hangul = 공작명왕 | hanja = | rr = Gongjak Myeongwang }} '''Mahamayuri''' ({{langx|sa|महामायूरी}} {{IAST|''Mahāmāyūrī''}} ("great peacock"), {{zh|孔雀明王}} ''Kǒngquè Míngwáng'', {{langx|vi|Khổng Tước Minh Vương}}, {{langx|ja|孔雀明王|''Kujaku Myōō''}}, {{langx|ko|공작명왕}} ''Gongjak Myeongwang''), or '''Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī''' is a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] and female [[Wisdom King]] in [[Mahayana]] and [[Vajrayana]] Buddhism. In the latter tradition, Mahamayuri is a popular practice in both the Chinese and Japanese forms of Vajrayana. She is also the name of one of the five protective goddesses in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003">{{cite book|author1=John C. Huntington|author2=Dina Bangdel|title=The Circle of Bliss: Buddhist Meditational Art|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l3KmWbcq5foC |year=2003|publisher=Serindia Publications |isbn=978-1-932476-01-9|pages=24–25}}</ref><ref name="Dalal2010p218">{{cite book|author=Roshen Dalal|title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&pg=PA218 |year=2010|publisher=Penguin |isbn=978-0-14-341517-6|page=218}}</ref><ref name="Shaw2006p233">{{cite book|author=Miranda Eberle Shaw|title=Buddhist Goddesses of India|url=https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw |url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-12758-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw/page/233 233], 339–341, 465 note 77}}</ref>
In Chinese Buddhism and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]], it is believed that the Great Peacock King is an incarnation of either [[Vairocana Buddha]] or [[Shakyamuni Buddha]]. Its main merits include the elimination of poison and disease, the protection of the country, the dispelling of disasters, and the ability to pray for both rain and cessation of rain.<ref>'''佛母五大明王'''（五位女性明王）指：大随求菩萨 大隨求明王、大千摧碎明王、隨持密咒明王、大寒林明王、大孔雀明王五位佛母；也就相對：《大隨求陀羅尼經》、《守護大千國土大明王陀羅尼經》、《大威德神呪經》、《尸多林經》、《佛母大孔雀明王經》五部經典。</ref>
==Name and origin== The Sanskrit name Mahāmāyūrī means 'great peahen'. Known as the 'Queen of the secret sciences' and the 'Godmother of Buddha',<ref>{{cite web|title=(孔雀明王咒) 的經典|url=http://paper.udn.com/udnpaper/POL0005/136113/web/|access-date=17 November 2017|work=paper.udn.com|language=zh}}</ref> Mahamayuri is believed to have the power to protect devotees from poisoning, either physical or spiritual. In Buddhism, her demeanor is in contrast to the wrathful attitudes of male personifications of the [[Wisdom King]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Art Institute of Chicago|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PfLzXKeVKzEC&pg=RA1-PA36|title=Notable Acquisitions at the Art Institute of Chicago|publisher=University of Illinois Press|year=2004|isbn=978-0-86559-209-4|page=36}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=True Buddha Dharma-character Treasury - Mahamayuri|url=http://tbsn.org/english2/news.php?id=469&classid=1|access-date=17 November 2017|work=tbsn.org|language=en}}</ref>
The ''Mahamayuri'' text is a Buddhist dharani-genre text, containing magical incantations to treat snake bites, poisons and other maladies.<ref>{{cite book|author=SKR Rao|title=Encyclopaedia of Indian Medicine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QRvzRGn911kC&pg=PA73|year=1985|publisher=Popular Prakashan|isbn=978-81-7154-255-0|page=73}}</ref> Mahamayuri's [[:en:Dharani|Dharani]] was translated into Chinese by [[:en:Kumārajīva|Kumārajīva]] between 402 and 412 CE.<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003"/> It contains the only mention of the [[Rig Veda]] in the entire [[:en:Chinese Buddhist canon|Chinese Buddhist canon]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Dalal |first1=Roshen |title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths |date=2010 |publisher=Penguin Books India |isbn=9780143415176 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&q=Mahamayuri+Chinese+Buddhist+canon&pg=PA218 |language=en}}</ref>
Her origins are said to derive from an Indian goddess of the same name. She is one of the Nepali [[pañcarakṣā]] deities, where she is the chief of the five rakṣā (守護) in the five parasol configuration, occupying the northern position. Unlike her four companions of the pañcarakṣā she appears to have had a fairly well-developed cult in India.<ref name="myoo">{{cite web |title=JAANUS / Kujaku Myouou 孔雀明王 |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kujakumyouou.htm |website=www.aisf.or.jp}}</ref>
==Iconography== [[File:Pancaraksa, AD 1653, Ranjana script, Mahamayun Wellcome L0035405.jpg|thumb|left| A miniature depicting Mahamayuri from the [[Pañcarakṣā]]]] Despite being associated with the [[Wisdom King]]s, Mahāmāyūrī tends to be portrayed with a benevolent expression rather than a wrathful one.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyohaku.go.jp/eng/dictio/kaiga/kujaku.html|title=About the Buddhist Painting, Peacock Myoo (Mahamayuri)|work=Kyoto National Museum|access-date=17 November 2017|language=en}}</ref> She has three faces and six hands.<ref name=v>{{cite web |title=Buddhist Deity: Pancha Raksha Main Page |url=https://www.himalayanart.org/search/set.cfm?setID=736 |website=Himalayan Art Resources}}</ref>
According to the ''Ritual for Painting the Image and Setting Up the Altar of the Great Peacock King Buddha Mother'' (大孔雀明王畫像壇場儀軌), Mahamayuri is depicted as a white human-headed peacock with four arms. She is often seated on a lotus flower or a peacock throne. The right hand holds a lotus flower, which represents the purity of his wisdom. The second right hand holds the fruit of all causes, which represents his ability to fulfill all wishes. The left hand holds a wish-fulfilling fruit, which represents his ability to grant all desires. The second left hand holds three or five peacock tails, which represent his power to dispel poison and disease. In the Garbhadhātu Mandala, Mahamayuri is enshrined in the sixth position of the southernmost corner of the {{ill|Susiddhivīja|ja|蘇悉地院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susiddhivīja|qq=}}.<ref name="legend"/>
She is often portrayed riding a peacock and commonly sporting four arms. Although the items she holds vary among traditions, common items include a [[:en:Citron|Citron]], [[:en:bael fruit|bael fruit]], lotus flower, and a peacock tail feather. With the moon as a background, wear peaceful ornaments and garments. Seated in the half (vajrasana) posture.<ref name=v/>
==Legends== [[File:孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō.jpg|thumb|孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō Image of Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī from the Taishō Shinshū Daizōkyō]] Legend holds that in primordial times, the original [[Phoenix (mythology)|Phoenix]] ([[:en:Fenghuang|Fenghuang]]), the leader of flying beings, gave birth to the peacock Mahamayuri and to the eagle named the [[:en:Dapeng Jinchi Mingwang|Golden-Winged Great Peng]]. The peacock once consumed the Buddha in one of his previous lives, who managed to escape by cutting through her stomach. At that time, the peacock preyed on humans, and the Buddha intended to kill it. However, the deities intervened and urged him to stop. In exchange for a promise to renounce its habit of preying on humans, the Buddha elevated the peacock to the status of his godmother, while the eagle became his uncle and was granted a high position in heaven.<ref>{{cite news |title=西遊最強姐弟，為何最後卻反目成仇，只因分別效忠兩位佛祖 |url=https://www.xuehua.us/2019/03/10/西游最强姐弟，为何最后却反目成仇，只因分别效/zh-tw/ |work=雪花新聞 |date=9 March 2019|language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊記中除了金翅大鵬，如來的親戚都是誰？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/CrkZgB |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=4 September 2017 |language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=9789866936333 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B+%E8%80%81%E9%B9%B0 |language=zh-TW}}</ref>
In the ''[[Mahamayuri Tantra]]'', it is stated that Mahamayuri assumes various forms to assist the Buddha, including taking on the human form of [[Queen Maya]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Mahamayuri Tantra |url=https://www.scribd.com/document/404209978/Mahamayuri-pdf |language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Kong Xuan|Kong Xuan]], a peacock spirit in the Ming Dynasty novel ''[[:en:Fengshen Yanyi|Fengshen Yanyi]]'', is based on the figure of Mahamayuri. Kong Xuan appears as a powerful general under [[:en:King Zhou of Shang|King Zhou of Shang]].<ref>{{cite book |title=中国精怪文化 [Chinese Ghost Culture] |date=1997 |publisher=上海人民出版社 |isbn=978-7-208-02454-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SSZhAAAAIAAJ&q=%E5%AD%94%E5%AE%A3+%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Journey to the West''=== The 16th-century Chinese novel ''[[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]]'' includes a story about how the king of the Kingdom of Zhuzi had a hobby of [[:en:Hunting|Hunting]] when he was a young prince. One day, he accidentally killed two followers of Mahamayuri. Later on, Mahamayuri said that to pay for this, the prince would suffer a lovesickness for 3 years. At the time, the bodhisattva [[Kannon|Guanyin]]'s steed [[:en:Denglong (mythology)|Golden Haired Hou]] went across the scene and managed to hear this. Years later, when the prince had grown up and became the king of the kingdom, the Golden Haired Hou took advantage of the child who guards him sleeping, escaped to the mortal realm, became the demon king Sai Tai Sui, and kidnapped the Lady of Jinsheng Palace (金聖宮娘娘), one of the king's favorite consorts, and forced her to marry him. Without the lady, the king got sick for three years.<ref>{{cite news |title=赛太岁很听话？观音菩萨"打诳语"有水平_手机搜狐网 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/107874164_109477/?pvid=000115_3w_a |work=Sohu|language=my|date=27 July 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊朱紫國王射傷了孔雀明王的孩子，為何是觀音坐騎出面復仇？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/Wjo7g5 |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=8 August 2017 |language=zh}}</ref>

Despite his brief time as a monk, he had already received the precepts and was diligently studying the Vinaya rules. While engaged in gathering firewood and preparing a bath for fellow monks, Sāḍdhi encountered a perilous situation when a large black snake emerged from a hole in a decaying tree and bit him on his right big toe. The venom swiftly spread throughout his body, causing him to collapse unconscious on the ground, foaming at the mouth, with his eyes rolled back.<ref name="legend">{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=978-986-6936-33-3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
[[:en:Ananda|Ananda]], witnessing the distressing scene, promptly sought the Buddha's assistance. In response, the Buddha informed Ananda about the potent mantra of the Mahamayuri (Great Peacock) Buddha Mother, emphasizing its ability to counteract poisons, dispel fears, avert calamities, and safeguard and nurture all sentient beings, leading them to happiness. The Buddha proceeded to impart the Mahamayuri Vidyarajni mantra to Ananda, who hastened back to Sāḍdhi and employed the mantra for healing. Through the power of the mantra, the poison was eradicated, and Sāḍdhi regained consciousness, fully restored to health. Expressing the significance of the mantra, the Buddha instructed Ananda to propagate it among all sentient beings, offering protection from harm and the opportunity to attain happiness. This mantra is reputed for its ability to safeguard against harm, alleviate ailments, and usher in happiness. Widely embraced by Mahayana Buddhism practitioners, the mantra is frequently chanted as a means of spiritual practice.<ref name="legend"/>
The Buddha imparted the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' with the specific intention of addressing the manifold challenges faced by sentient beings. According to the sutra, both the text and the mantra possess the extraordinary power to alleviate diseases, afflictions, and fears while serving as a protective shield for all living beings. Instructing Ananda to disseminate this sutra and mantra widely, the Buddha aimed to liberate sentient beings from suffering and guide them towards the attainment of happiness.<ref name="legend"/>
Delving into the origin of the Buddha's emphasis on this mantra, the sutra recounts a past life where the Buddha existed as a golden peacock king residing on the southern slope of [[Mount Meru]]. Engaging in the daily recitation of the mantra, the king experienced perpetual peace in mind and body. However, a lapse occurred when, driven by desire, he neglected the mantra to frolic with his peacock consorts in the forest. Succumbing to intoxication and a loss of mindfulness, he inadvertently fell into a hunter's trap. Bound and facing peril, he remembered the mantra, reciting it and miraculously securing his release, with his consorts emerging unscathed.<ref name="legend"/>
Recognizing the formidable efficacy of this mantra, the Buddha discerned its capacity to mitigate grave transgressions, eradicate minor wrongdoings, and dispel all forms of suffering. Out of compassion, the Buddha shared this transformative mantra with all sentient beings, envisioning that its constant recitation would empower individuals to transcend fear, surmount suffering, perpetually attract good fortune, evade danger, and live long and contented lives.<ref name="legend"/>
== In Chinese Buddhism == In China, she is known as Kǒngquè Míngwáng (孔雀明王) and her image is enshrined in many Chinese Buddhist temples. In contemporary times, her veneration is thought to be closely related to medical care, public health, environmental protection, and social welfare.
In [[:en:China|China]], some of the temples which venerate her include the [[Luohan Temple (Chongqing)|Luohan Temple]] in [[:en:Chongqing|Chongqing]], [[Wuyou Temple]], [[:en:Baoguang Temple|Baoguang Temple]] and [[:en:Fuhu Temple|Fuhu Temple]] in [[Sichuan]], [[Shifo Temple]] in [[Shaanxi]], [http://www.jgms.net/ Jinguangming Temple] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], as well as [[Qiongzhu Temple]] in [[Yunnan]]. In [[Taiwan]], her veneration is promoted by the [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about.html Chinese Great Peacock Buddha Association](中華世界大孔雀佛協會), which has their main headquarters located at [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about01.html Kaicheng Temple]. In [[Singapore]], one temple which venerates her is [[:en:Hua Giam Si|Hua Gim Si Temple]].
==In Japanese Buddhism== In Japan she was known as the name Kujaku Myōō (孔雀明王). There is a record stating that she was worshiped during the [[Nara period]], and her image was placed at the newly constructed [[Saidai-ji]] Temple Kondo (Saidai-ji Temple Golden Hall).<ref name="myoo"/>
== Gallery == <gallery> Banner with Bodhisattva, possibly Mahamayuri.jpg| Chinesischer Maler des 11. Jahrhunderts (II) 001.jpg| Dazu 2007 665.jpg| Mahamayuri statue.jpg| LK-colombo-gangaramaya-pfau.jpg| </gallery>

revision.text:
{{short description|Bodhisattva and female Wisdom King}} {{Chinese | title = Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī | pic = Kujaku Myoo edit.jpg | picsize = | piccap = Painting of Mahāmāyūrī, pigment on silk. Japan, [[Heian Period]], 12th century CE | san = {{IAST|Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī}} | l = Peacock Wisdom Queen | t = 孔雀明王 | s = 孔雀明王 | p = Kǒngquè Míngwáng | w = | mi = | vie = Khổng Tước Minh Vương | kanji = 孔雀明王 | romaji = Kujaku Myōō | hangul = 공작명왕 | hanja = | rr = Gongjak Myeongwang }} '''Mahamayuri''' ({{langx|sa|महामायूरी}} {{IAST|''Mahāmāyūrī''}} ("great peacock"), {{zh|孔雀明王}} ''Kǒngquè Míngwáng'', {{langx|vi|Khổng Tước Minh Vương}}, {{langx|ja|孔雀明王|''Kujaku Myōō''}}, {{langx|ko|공작명왕}} ''Gongjak Myeongwang''), or '''Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī''' is a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] and female [[Wisdom King]] in [[Mahayana]] and [[Vajrayana]] Buddhism. In the latter tradition, Mahamayuri is a popular practice in both the Chinese and Japanese forms of Vajrayana. She is also the name of one of the five protective goddesses in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003">{{cite book|author1=John C. Huntington|author2=Dina Bangdel|title=The Circle of Bliss: Buddhist Meditational Art|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l3KmWbcq5foC |year=2003|publisher=Serindia Publications |isbn=978-1-932476-01-9|pages=24–25}}</ref><ref name="Dalal2010p218">{{cite book|author=Roshen Dalal|title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&pg=PA218 |year=2010|publisher=Penguin |isbn=978-0-14-341517-6|page=218}}</ref><ref name="Shaw2006p233">{{cite book|author=Miranda Eberle Shaw|title=Buddhist Goddesses of India|url=https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw |url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-12758-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw/page/233 233], 339–341, 465 note 77}}</ref>
In Chinese Buddhism and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]], it is believed that the Great Peacock King is an incarnation of either [[Vairocana Buddha]] or [[Shakyamuni Buddha]]. Its main merits include the elimination of poison and disease, the protection of the country, the dispelling of disasters, and the ability to pray for both rain and cessation of rain.<ref>'''佛母五大明王'''（五位女性明王）指：大随求菩萨 大隨求明王、大千摧碎明王、隨持密咒明王、大寒林明王、大孔雀明王五位佛母；也就相對：《大隨求陀羅尼經》、《守護大千國土大明王陀羅尼經》、《大威德神呪經》、《尸多林經》、《佛母大孔雀明王經》五部經典。</ref>
==Name and origin== The Sanskrit name Mahāmāyūrī means 'great peahen'. Known as the 'Queen of the secret sciences' and the 'Godmother of Buddha',<ref>{{cite web|title=(孔雀明王咒) 的經典|url=http://paper.udn.com/udnpaper/POL0005/136113/web/|access-date=17 November 2017|work=paper.udn.com|language=zh}}</ref> Mahamayuri is believed to have the power to protect devotees from poisoning, either physical or spiritual. In Buddhism, her demeanor is in contrast to the wrathful attitudes of male personifications of the [[Wisdom King]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Art Institute of Chicago|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PfLzXKeVKzEC&pg=RA1-PA36|title=Notable Acquisitions at the Art Institute of Chicago|publisher=University of Illinois Press|year=2004|isbn=978-0-86559-209-4|page=36}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=True Buddha Dharma-character Treasury - Mahamayuri|url=http://tbsn.org/english2/news.php?id=469&classid=1|access-date=17 November 2017|work=tbsn.org|language=en}}</ref>
The ''Mahamayuri'' text is a Buddhist dharani-genre text, containing magical incantations to treat snake bites, poisons and other maladies.<ref>{{cite book|author=SKR Rao|title=Encyclopaedia of Indian Medicine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QRvzRGn911kC&pg=PA73|year=1985|publisher=Popular Prakashan|isbn=978-81-7154-255-0|page=73}}</ref> Mahamayuri's [[:en:Dharani|Dharani]] was translated into Chinese by [[:en:Kumārajīva|Kumārajīva]] between 402 and 412 CE.<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003"/> It contains the only mention of the [[Rig Veda]] in the entire [[:en:Chinese Buddhist canon|Chinese Buddhist canon]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Dalal |first1=Roshen |title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths |date=2010 |publisher=Penguin Books India |isbn=9780143415176 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&q=Mahamayuri+Chinese+Buddhist+canon&pg=PA218 |language=en}}</ref>
Her origins are said to derive from an Indian goddess of the same name. She is one of the Nepali [[pañcarakṣā]] deities, where she is the chief of the five rakṣā (守護) in the five parasol configuration, occupying the northern position. Unlike her four companions of the pañcarakṣā she appears to have had a fairly well-developed cult in India.<ref name="myoo">{{cite web |title=JAANUS / Kujaku Myouou 孔雀明王 |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kujakumyouou.htm |website=www.aisf.or.jp}}</ref>
==Iconography== [[File:Pancaraksa, AD 1653, Ranjana script, Mahamayun Wellcome L0035405.jpg|thumb|left| A miniature depicting Mahamayuri from the [[Pañcarakṣā]]]] Despite being associated with the [[Wisdom King]]s, Mahāmāyūrī tends to be portrayed with a benevolent expression rather than a wrathful one.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyohaku.go.jp/eng/dictio/kaiga/kujaku.html|title=About the Buddhist Painting, Peacock Myoo (Mahamayuri)|work=Kyoto National Museum|access-date=17 November 2017|language=en}}</ref> She has three faces and six hands.<ref name=v>{{cite web |title=Buddhist Deity: Pancha Raksha Main Page |url=https://www.himalayanart.org/search/set.cfm?setID=736 |website=Himalayan Art Resources}}</ref>
According to the ''Ritual for Painting the Image and Setting Up the Altar of the Great Peacock King Buddha Mother'' (大孔雀明王畫像壇場儀軌), Mahamayuri is depicted as a white human-headed peacock with four arms. She is often seated on a lotus flower or a peacock throne. The right hand holds a lotus flower, which represents the purity of his wisdom. The second right hand holds the fruit of all causes, which represents his ability to fulfill all wishes. The left hand holds a wish-fulfilling fruit, which represents his ability to grant all desires. The second left hand holds three or five peacock tails, which represent his power to dispel poison and disease. In the Garbhadhātu Mandala, Mahamayuri is enshrined in the sixth position of the southernmost corner of the {{ill|Susiddhivīja|ja|蘇悉地院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susiddhivīja|qq=}}.<ref name="legend"/>
She is often portrayed riding a peacock and commonly sporting four arms. Although the items she holds vary among traditions, common items include a [[:en:Citron|Citron]], [[:en:bael fruit|bael fruit]], lotus flower, and a peacock tail feather. With the moon as a background, wear peaceful ornaments and garments. Seated in the half (vajrasana) posture.<ref name=v/>
==Legends== [[File:孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō.jpg|thumb|孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō Image of Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī from the Taishō Shinshū Daizōkyō]] Legend holds that in primordial times, the original [[Phoenix (mythology)|Phoenix]] ([[:en:Fenghuang|Fenghuang]]), the leader of flying beings, gave birth to the peacock Mahamayuri and to the eagle named the [[:en:Dapeng Jinchi Mingwang|Golden-Winged Great Peng]]. The peacock once consumed the Buddha in one of his previous lives, who managed to escape by cutting through her stomach. At that time, the peacock preyed on humans, and the Buddha intended to kill it. However, the deities intervened and urged him to stop. In exchange for a promise to renounce its habit of preying on humans, the Buddha elevated the peacock to the status of his godmother, while the eagle became his uncle and was granted a high position in heaven.<ref>{{cite news |title=西遊最強姐弟，為何最後卻反目成仇，只因分別效忠兩位佛祖 |url=https://www.xuehua.us/2019/03/10/西游最强姐弟，为何最后却反目成仇，只因分别效/zh-tw/ |work=雪花新聞 |date=9 March 2019|language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊記中除了金翅大鵬，如來的親戚都是誰？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/CrkZgB |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=4 September 2017 |language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=9789866936333 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B+%E8%80%81%E9%B9%B0 |language=zh-TW}}</ref>
In the ''[[Mahamayuri Tantra]]'', it is stated that Mahamayuri assumes various forms to assist the Buddha, including taking on the human form of [[Queen Maya]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Mahamayuri Tantra |url=https://www.scribd.com/document/404209978/Mahamayuri-pdf |language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Kong Xuan|Kong Xuan]], a peacock spirit in the Ming Dynasty novel ''[[:en:Fengshen Yanyi|Fengshen Yanyi]]'', is based on the figure of Mahamayuri. Kong Xuan appears as a powerful general under [[:en:King Zhou of Shang|King Zhou of Shang]].<ref>{{cite book |title=中国精怪文化 [Chinese Ghost Culture] |date=1997 |publisher=上海人民出版社 |isbn=978-7-208-02454-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SSZhAAAAIAAJ&q=%E5%AD%94%E5%AE%A3+%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Journey to the West''=== The 16th-century Chinese novel ''[[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]]'' includes a story about how the king of the Kingdom of Zhuzi had a hobby of [[:en:Hunting|Hunting]] when he was a young prince. One day, he accidentally killed two followers of Mahamayuri. Later on, Mahamayuri said that to pay for this, the prince would suffer a lovesickness for 3 years. At the time, the bodhisattva [[Kannon|Guanyin]]'s steed [[:en:Denglong (mythology)|Golden Haired Hou]] went across the scene and managed to hear this. Years later, when the prince had grown up and became the king of the kingdom, the Golden Haired Hou took advantage of the child who guards him sleeping, escaped to the mortal realm, became the demon king Sai Tai Sui, and kidnapped the Lady of Jinsheng Palace (金聖宮娘娘), one of the king's favorite consorts, and forced her to marry him. Without the lady, the king got sick for three years.<ref>{{cite news |title=赛太岁很听话？观音菩萨"打诳语"有水平_手机搜狐网 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/107874164_109477/?pvid=000115_3w_a |work=Sohu|language=my|date=27 July 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊朱紫國王射傷了孔雀明王的孩子，為何是觀音坐騎出面復仇？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/Wjo7g5 |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=8 August 2017 |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra''=== According to the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'', during the time of the Buddha, there was a recently ordained monk named Sāḍdhi. Despite his brief time as a monk, he had already received the precepts and was diligently studying the Vinaya rules. While engaged in gathering firewood and preparing a bath for fellow monks, Sāḍdhi encountered a perilous situation when a large black snake emerged from a hole in a decaying tree and bit him on his right big toe. The venom swiftly spread throughout his body, causing him to collapse unconscious on the ground, foaming at the mouth, with his eyes rolled back.<ref name="legend">{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=978-986-6936-33-3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
[[:en:Ananda|Ananda]], witnessing the distressing scene, promptly sought the Buddha's assistance. In response, the Buddha informed Ananda about the potent mantra of the Mahamayuri (Great Peacock) Buddha Mother, emphasizing its ability to counteract poisons, dispel fears, avert calamities, and safeguard and nurture all sentient beings, leading them to happiness. The Buddha proceeded to impart the Mahamayuri Vidyarajni mantra to Ananda, who hastened back to Sāḍdhi and employed the mantra for healing. Through the power of the mantra, the poison was eradicated, and Sāḍdhi regained consciousness, fully restored to health. Expressing the significance of the mantra, the Buddha instructed Ananda to propagate it among all sentient beings, offering protection from harm and the opportunity to attain happiness. The ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' is a Mahayana Buddhist scripture that imparts the mantra of the Mahamayuri Buddha Mother. This mantra is reputed for its ability to safeguard against harm, alleviate ailments, and usher in happiness. Widely embraced by Mahayana Buddhism practitioners, the mantra is frequently chanted as a means of spiritual practice.<ref name="legend"/>
The Buddha imparted the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' with the specific intention of addressing the manifold challenges faced by sentient beings. According to the sutra, both the text and the mantra possess the extraordinary power to alleviate diseases, afflictions, and fears while serving as a protective shield for all living beings. Instructing Ananda to disseminate this sutra and mantra widely, the Buddha aimed to liberate sentient beings from suffering and guide them towards the attainment of happiness.<ref name="legend"/>
Delving into the origin of the Buddha's emphasis on this mantra, the sutra recounts a past life where the Buddha existed as a golden peacock king residing on the southern slope of [[Mount Meru]]. Engaging in the daily recitation of the mantra, the king experienced perpetual peace in mind and body. However, a lapse occurred when, driven by desire, he neglected the mantra to frolic with his peacock consorts in the forest. Succumbing to intoxication and a loss of mindfulness, he inadvertently fell into a hunter's trap. Bound and facing peril, he remembered the mantra, reciting it and miraculously securing his release, with his consorts emerging unscathed.<ref name="legend"/>
Recognizing the formidable efficacy of this mantra, the Buddha discerned its capacity to mitigate grave transgressions, eradicate minor wrongdoings, and dispel all forms of suffering. Out of compassion, the Buddha shared this transformative mantra with all sentient beings, envisioning that its constant recitation would empower individuals to transcend fear, surmount suffering, perpetually attract good fortune, evade danger, and live long and contented lives.<ref name="legend"/>
== In Chinese Buddhism == In China, she is known as Kǒngquè Míngwáng (孔雀明王) and her image is enshrined in many Chinese Buddhist temples. In contemporary times, her veneration is thought to be closely related to medical care, public health, environmental protection, and social welfare.
In [[:en:China|China]], some of the temples which venerate her include the [[:en:Luohan Temple (Chongqing)|Luohan Temple]] in [[:en:Chongqing|Chongqing]], [[Wuyou Temple]], [[:en:Baoguang Temple|Baoguang Temple]] and [[:en:Fuhu Temple|Fuhu Temple]] in [[Sichuan]], [[Shifo Temple]] in [[Shaanxi]], [http://www.jgms.net/ Jinguangming Temple] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], as well as [[Qiongzhu Temple]] in [[Yunnan]]. In [[Taiwan]], her veneration is promoted by the [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about.html Chinese Great Peacock Buddha Association](中華世界大孔雀佛協會), which has their main headquarters located at [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about01.html Kaicheng Temple]. In [[Singapore]], one temple which venerates her is [[:en:Hua Giam Si|Hua Gim Si Temple]].
==In Japanese Buddhism== In Japan she was known as the name Kujaku Myōō (孔雀明王). There is a record stating that she was worshiped during the [[Nara period]], and her image was placed at the newly constructed [[Saidai-ji]] Temple Kondo (Saidai-ji Temple Golden Hall).<ref name="myoo"/>
== Gallery == <gallery> Banner with Bodhisattva, possibly Mahamayuri.jpg| Chinesischer Maler des 11. Jahrhunderts (II) 001.jpg| Dazu 2007 665.jpg| Mahamayuri statue.jpg| LK-colombo-gangaramaya-pfau.jpg| </gallery>

Japan, [[Heian Period]], 12th century CE | san = {{IAST|Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī}} | l = Peacock Wisdom Queen | t = 孔雀明王 | s = 孔雀明王 | p = Kǒngquè Míngwáng | w = | mi = | vie = Khổng Tước Minh Vương | kanji = 孔雀明王 | romaji = Kujaku Myōō | hangul = 공작명왕 | hanja = | rr = Gongjak Myeongwang }} '''Mahamayuri''' ({{langx|sa|महामायूरी}} {{IAST|''Mahāmāyūrī''}} ("great peacock"), {{zh|孔雀明王}} ''Kǒngquè Míngwáng'', {{langx|vi|Khổng Tước Minh Vương}}, {{langx|ja|孔雀明王|''Kujaku Myōō''}}, {{langx|ko|공작명왕}} ''Gongjak Myeongwang''), or '''Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī''' is a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] and female [[Wisdom King]] in [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] and [[Vajrayana]] Buddhism. In the latter tradition, Mahamayuri is a popular practice in both the Chinese and Japanese forms of Vajrayana. She is also the name of one of the five protective goddesses in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003">{{cite book|author1=John C. Huntington|author2=Dina Bangdel|title=The Circle of Bliss: Buddhist Meditational Art|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l3KmWbcq5foC |year=2003|publisher=Serindia Publications |isbn=978-1-932476-01-9|pages=24–25}}</ref><ref name="Dalal2010p218">{{cite book|author=Roshen Dalal|title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&pg=PA218 |year=2010|publisher=Penguin |isbn=978-0-14-341517-6|page=218}}</ref><ref name="Shaw2006p233">{{cite book|author=Miranda Eberle Shaw|title=Buddhist Goddesses of India|url=https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw |url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-12758-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw/page/233 233], 339–341, 465 note 77}}</ref>
In Chinese Buddhism and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]], it is believed that the Great Peacock King is an incarnation of either [[Vairocana Buddha]] or [[Shakyamuni Buddha]]. Its main merits include the elimination of poison and disease, the protection of the country, the dispelling of disasters, and the ability to pray for both rain and cessation of rain.<ref>'''佛母五大明王'''（五位女性明王）指：大随求菩萨 大隨求明王、大千摧碎明王、隨持密咒明王、大寒林明王、大孔雀明王五位佛母；也就相對：《大隨求陀羅尼經》、《守護大千國土大明王陀羅尼經》、《大威德神呪經》、《尸多林經》、《佛母大孔雀明王經》五部經典。</ref>
==Name and origin== The Sanskrit name Mahāmāyūrī means 'great peahen'. Known as the 'Queen of the secret sciences' and the 'Godmother of Buddha',<ref>{{cite web|title=(孔雀明王咒) 的經典|url=http://paper.udn.com/udnpaper/POL0005/136113/web/|access-date=17 November 2017|work=paper.udn.com|language=zh}}</ref> Mahamayuri is believed to have the power to protect devotees from poisoning, either physical or spiritual. In Buddhism, her demeanor is in contrast to the wrathful attitudes of male personifications of the [[Wisdom King]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Art Institute of Chicago|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PfLzXKeVKzEC&pg=RA1-PA36|title=Notable Acquisitions at the Art Institute of Chicago|publisher=University of Illinois Press|year=2004|isbn=978-0-86559-209-4|page=36}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=True Buddha Dharma-character Treasury - Mahamayuri|url=http://tbsn.org/english2/news.php?id=469&classid=1|access-date=17 November 2017|work=tbsn.org|language=en}}</ref>
The ''Mahamayuri'' text is a Buddhist dharani-genre text, containing magical incantations to treat snake bites, poisons and other maladies.<ref>{{cite book|author=SKR Rao|title=Encyclopaedia of Indian Medicine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QRvzRGn911kC&pg=PA73|year=1985|publisher=Popular Prakashan|isbn=978-81-7154-255-0|page=73}}</ref> Mahamayuri's [[:en:Dharani|Dharani]] was translated into Chinese by [[:en:Kumārajīva|Kumārajīva]] between 402 and 412 CE.<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003"/> It contains the only mention of the [[Rig Veda]] in the entire [[:en:Chinese Buddhist canon|Chinese Buddhist canon]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Dalal |first1=Roshen |title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths |date=2010 |publisher=Penguin Books India |isbn=9780143415176 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&q=Mahamayuri+Chinese+Buddhist+canon&pg=PA218 |language=en}}</ref>
Her origins are said to derive from an Indian goddess of the same name. She is one of the Nepali [[pañcarakṣā]] deities, where she is the chief of the five rakṣā (守護) in the five parasol configuration, occupying the northern position. Unlike her four companions of the pañcarakṣā she appears to have had a fairly well-developed cult in India.<ref name="myoo">{{cite web |title=JAANUS / Kujaku Myouou 孔雀明王 |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kujakumyouou.htm |website=www.aisf.or.jp}}</ref>
==Iconography== [[File:Pancaraksa, AD 1653, Ranjana script, Mahamayun Wellcome L0035405.jpg|thumb|left| A miniature depicting Mahamayuri from the [[Pañcarakṣā]]]] Despite being associated with the [[Wisdom King]]s, Mahāmāyūrī tends to be portrayed with a benevolent expression rather than a wrathful one.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyohaku.go.jp/eng/dictio/kaiga/kujaku.html|title=About the Buddhist Painting, Peacock Myoo (Mahamayuri)|work=Kyoto National Museum|access-date=17 November 2017|language=en}}</ref> She has three faces and six hands.<ref name=v>{{cite web |title=Buddhist Deity: Pancha Raksha Main Page |url=https://www.himalayanart.org/search/set.cfm?setID=736 |website=Himalayan Art Resources}}</ref>
According to the ''Ritual for Painting the Image and Setting Up the Altar of the Great Peacock King Buddha Mother'' (大孔雀明王畫像壇場儀軌), Mahamayuri is depicted as a white human-headed peacock with four arms. She is often seated on a lotus flower or a peacock throne. The right hand holds a lotus flower, which represents the purity of his wisdom. The second right hand holds the fruit of all causes, which represents his ability to fulfill all wishes. The left hand holds a wish-fulfilling fruit, which represents his ability to grant all desires. The second left hand holds three or five peacock tails, which represent his power to dispel poison and disease. In the Garbhadhātu Mandala, Mahamayuri is enshrined in the sixth position of the southernmost corner of the {{ill|Susiddhivīja|ja|蘇悉地院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susiddhivīja|qq=}}.<ref name="legend"/>
She is often portrayed riding a peacock and commonly sporting four arms. Although the items she holds vary among traditions, common items include a [[:en:Citron|Citron]], [[:en:bael fruit|bael fruit]], lotus flower, and a peacock tail feather. With the moon as a background, wear peaceful ornaments and garments. Seated in the half (vajrasana) posture.<ref name=v/>
==Legends== [[File:孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō.jpg|thumb|孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō Image of Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī from the Taishō Shinshū Daizōkyō]] Legend holds that in primordial times, the original [[Phoenix (mythology)|Phoenix]] ([[:en:Fenghuang|Fenghuang]]), the leader of flying beings, gave birth to the peacock Mahamayuri and to the eagle named the [[:en:Dapeng Jinchi Mingwang|Golden-Winged Great Peng]]. The peacock once consumed the Buddha in one of his previous lives, who managed to escape by cutting through her stomach. At that time, the peacock preyed on humans, and the Buddha intended to kill it. However, the deities intervened and urged him to stop. In exchange for a promise to renounce its habit of preying on humans, the Buddha elevated the peacock to the status of his godmother, while the eagle became his uncle and was granted a high position in heaven.<ref>{{cite news |title=西遊最強姐弟，為何最後卻反目成仇，只因分別效忠兩位佛祖 |url=https://www.xuehua.us/2019/03/10/西游最强姐弟，为何最后却反目成仇，只因分别效/zh-tw/ |work=雪花新聞 |date=9 March 2019|language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊記中除了金翅大鵬，如來的親戚都是誰？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/CrkZgB |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=4 September 2017 |language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=9789866936333 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B+%E8%80%81%E9%B9%B0 |language=zh-TW}}</ref>
In the ''[[Mahamayuri Tantra]]'', it is stated that Mahamayuri assumes various forms to assist the Buddha, including taking on the human form of [[Queen Maya]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Mahamayuri Tantra |url=https://www.scribd.com/document/404209978/Mahamayuri-pdf |language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Kong Xuan|Kong Xuan]], a peacock spirit in the Ming Dynasty novel ''[[:en:Fengshen Yanyi|Fengshen Yanyi]]'', is based on the figure of Mahamayuri. Kong Xuan appears as a powerful general under [[:en:King Zhou of Shang|King Zhou of Shang]].<ref>{{cite book |title=中国精怪文化 [Chinese Ghost Culture] |date=1997 |publisher=上海人民出版社 |isbn=978-7-208-02454-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SSZhAAAAIAAJ&q=%E5%AD%94%E5%AE%A3+%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Journey to the West''=== The 16th-century Chinese novel ''[[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]]'' includes a story about how the king of the Kingdom of Zhuzi had a hobby of [[:en:Hunting|Hunting]] when he was a young prince. One day, he accidentally killed two followers of Mahamayuri. Later on, Mahamayuri said that to pay for this, the prince would suffer a lovesickness for 3 years. At the time, the bodhisattva [[Kannon|Guanyin]]'s steed [[:en:Denglong (mythology)|Golden Haired Hou]] went across the scene and managed to hear this. Years later, when the prince had grown up and became the king of the kingdom, the Golden Haired Hou took advantage of the child who guards him sleeping, escaped to the mortal realm, became the demon king Sai Tai Sui, and kidnapped the Lady of Jinsheng Palace (金聖宮娘娘), one of the king's favorite consorts, and forced her to marry him. Without the lady, the king got sick for three years.<ref>{{cite news |title=赛太岁很听话？观音菩萨"打诳语"有水平_手机搜狐网 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/107874164_109477/?pvid=000115_3w_a |work=Sohu|language=my|date=27 July 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊朱紫國王射傷了孔雀明王的孩子，為何是觀音坐騎出面復仇？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/Wjo7g5 |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=8 August 2017 |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra''=== According to the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'', during the time of the Buddha, there was a recently ordained monk named Sāḍdhi. Despite his brief time as a monk, he had already received the precepts and was diligently studying the Vinaya rules. While engaged in gathering firewood and preparing a bath for fellow monks, Sāḍdhi encountered a perilous situation when a large black snake emerged from a hole in a decaying tree and bit him on his right big toe. The venom swiftly spread throughout his body, causing him to collapse unconscious on the ground, foaming at the mouth, with his eyes rolled back.<ref name="legend">{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=978-986-6936-33-3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
[[:en:Ananda|Ananda]], witnessing the distressing scene, promptly sought the Buddha's assistance. In response, the Buddha informed Ananda about the potent mantra of the Mahamayuri (Great Peacock) Buddha Mother, emphasizing its ability to counteract poisons, dispel fears, avert calamities, and safeguard and nurture all sentient beings, leading them to happiness. The Buddha proceeded to impart the Mahamayuri Vidyarajni mantra to Ananda, who hastened back to Sāḍdhi and employed the mantra for healing. Through the power of the mantra, the poison was eradicated, and Sāḍdhi regained consciousness, fully restored to health. Expressing the significance of the mantra, the Buddha instructed Ananda to propagate it among all sentient beings, offering protection from harm and the opportunity to attain happiness. The ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' is a Mahayana Buddhist scripture that imparts the mantra of the Mahamayuri Buddha Mother. This mantra is reputed for its ability to safeguard against harm, alleviate ailments, and usher in happiness. Widely embraced by Mahayana Buddhism practitioners, the mantra is frequently chanted as a means of spiritual practice.<ref name="legend"/>
The Buddha imparted the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' with the specific intention of addressing the manifold challenges faced by sentient beings. According to the sutra, both the text and the mantra possess the extraordinary power to alleviate diseases, afflictions, and fears while serving as a protective shield for all living beings. Instructing Ananda to disseminate this sutra and mantra widely, the Buddha aimed to liberate sentient beings from suffering and guide them towards the attainment of happiness.<ref name="legend"/>
Delving into the origin of the Buddha's emphasis on this mantra, the sutra recounts a past life where the Buddha existed as a golden peacock king residing on the southern slope of [[Mount Meru]]. Engaging in the daily recitation of the mantra, the king experienced perpetual peace in mind and body. However, a lapse occurred when, driven by desire, he neglected the mantra to frolic with his peacock consorts in the forest. Succumbing to intoxication and a loss of mindfulness, he inadvertently fell into a hunter's trap. Bound and facing peril, he remembered the mantra, reciting it and miraculously securing his release, with his consorts emerging unscathed.<ref name="legend"/>
Recognizing the formidable efficacy of this mantra, the Buddha discerned its capacity to mitigate grave transgressions, eradicate minor wrongdoings, and dispel all forms of suffering. Out of compassion, the Buddha shared this transformative mantra with all sentient beings, envisioning that its constant recitation would empower individuals to transcend fear, surmount suffering, perpetually attract good fortune, evade danger, and live long and contented lives.<ref name="legend"/>
== In Chinese Buddhism == In China, she is known as Kǒngquè Míngwáng (孔雀明王) and her image is enshrined in many Chinese Buddhist temples. In contemporary times, her veneration is thought to be closely related to medical care, public health, environmental protection, and social welfare.
In [[:en:China|China]], some of the temples which venerate her include the [[:en:Luohan Temple (Chongqing)|Luohan Temple]] in [[:en:Chongqing|Chongqing]], [[Wuyou Temple]], [[:en:Baoguang Temple|Baoguang Temple]] and [[:en:Fuhu Temple|Fuhu Temple]] in [[Sichuan]], [[Shifo Temple]] in [[Shaanxi]], [http://www.jgms.net/ Jinguangming Temple] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], as well as [[Qiongzhu Temple]] in [[Yunnan]]. In [[Taiwan]], her veneration is promoted by the [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about.html Chinese Great Peacock Buddha Association](中華世界大孔雀佛協會), which has their main headquarters located at [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about01.html Kaicheng Temple]. In [[Singapore]], one temple which venerates her is [[:en:Hua Giam Si|Hua Gim Si Temple]].
==In Japanese Buddhism== In Japan she was known as the name Kujaku Myōō (孔雀明王). There is a record stating that she was worshiped during the [[Nara period]], and her image was placed at the newly constructed [[Saidai-ji]] Temple Kondo (Saidai-ji Temple Golden Hall).<ref name="myoo"/>
== Gallery == <gallery> Banner with Bodhisattva, possibly Mahamayuri.jpg| Chinesischer Maler des 11. Jahrhunderts (II) 001.jpg| Dazu 2007 665.jpg| Mahamayuri statue.jpg| LK-colombo-gangaramaya-pfau.jpg| </gallery>
==See also== * [[:en:Chinese Buddhism|Chinese Buddhism]] *[[:en:Chinese gods and immortals|Chinese gods and immortals]] * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

revision.text:
{{short description|Bodhisattva and female Wisdom King}} {{Chinese | title = Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī | pic = Kujaku Myoo edit.jpg | picsize = | piccap = Painting of Mahāmāyūrī, pigment on silk. Japan, [[Heian Period]], 12th century CE | san = {{IAST|Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī}} | l = Peacock Wisdom Queen | t = 孔雀明王 | s = 孔雀明王 | p = Kǒngquè Míngwáng | w = | mi = | vie = Khổng Tước Minh Vương | kanji = 孔雀明王 | romaji = Kujaku Myōō | hangul = 공작명왕 | hanja = | rr = Gongjak Myeongwang }} '''Mahamayuri''' ({{langx|sa|महामायूरी}} {{IAST|''Mahāmāyūrī''}} ("great peacock"), {{zh|孔雀明王}} ''Kǒngquè Míngwáng'', {{langx|vi|Khổng Tước Minh Vương}}, {{langx|ja|孔雀明王|''Kujaku Myōō''}}, {{langx|ko|공작명왕}} ''Gongjak Myeongwang''), or '''Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī''' is a [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattva]] and female [[Wisdom King]] in [[:en:Mahayana|Mahayana]] and [[Vajrayana]] Buddhism. In the latter tradition, Mahamayuri is a popular practice in both the Chinese and Japanese forms of Vajrayana. She is also the name of one of the five protective goddesses in [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003">{{cite book|author1=John C. Huntington|author2=Dina Bangdel|title=The Circle of Bliss: Buddhist Meditational Art|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=l3KmWbcq5foC |year=2003|publisher=Serindia Publications |isbn=978-1-932476-01-9|pages=24–25}}</ref><ref name="Dalal2010p218">{{cite book|author=Roshen Dalal|title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&pg=PA218 |year=2010|publisher=Penguin |isbn=978-0-14-341517-6|page=218}}</ref><ref name="Shaw2006p233">{{cite book|author=Miranda Eberle Shaw|title=Buddhist Goddesses of India|url=https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw |url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0-691-12758-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/buddhistgoddesse00shaw/page/233 233], 339–341, 465 note 77}}</ref>
In Chinese Buddhism and [[:en:Shingon Buddhism|Shingon Buddhism]], it is believed that the Great Peacock King is an incarnation of either [[Vairocana Buddha]] or [[Shakyamuni Buddha]]. Its main merits include the elimination of poison and disease, the protection of the country, the dispelling of disasters, and the ability to pray for both rain and cessation of rain.<ref>'''佛母五大明王'''（五位女性明王）指：大随求菩萨 大隨求明王、大千摧碎明王、隨持密咒明王、大寒林明王、大孔雀明王五位佛母；也就相對：《大隨求陀羅尼經》、《守護大千國土大明王陀羅尼經》、《大威德神呪經》、《尸多林經》、《佛母大孔雀明王經》五部經典。</ref>
==Name and origin== The Sanskrit name Mahāmāyūrī means 'great peahen'. Known as the 'Queen of the secret sciences' and the 'Godmother of Buddha',<ref>{{cite web|title=(孔雀明王咒) 的經典|url=http://paper.udn.com/udnpaper/POL0005/136113/web/|access-date=17 November 2017|work=paper.udn.com|language=zh}}</ref> Mahamayuri is believed to have the power to protect devotees from poisoning, either physical or spiritual. In Buddhism, her demeanor is in contrast to the wrathful attitudes of male personifications of the [[Wisdom King]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Art Institute of Chicago|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PfLzXKeVKzEC&pg=RA1-PA36|title=Notable Acquisitions at the Art Institute of Chicago|publisher=University of Illinois Press|year=2004|isbn=978-0-86559-209-4|page=36}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=True Buddha Dharma-character Treasury - Mahamayuri|url=http://tbsn.org/english2/news.php?id=469&classid=1|access-date=17 November 2017|work=tbsn.org|language=en}}</ref>
The ''Mahamayuri'' text is a Buddhist dharani-genre text, containing magical incantations to treat snake bites, poisons and other maladies.<ref>{{cite book|author=SKR Rao|title=Encyclopaedia of Indian Medicine |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QRvzRGn911kC&pg=PA73|year=1985|publisher=Popular Prakashan|isbn=978-81-7154-255-0|page=73}}</ref> Mahamayuri's [[:en:Dharani|Dharani]] was translated into Chinese by [[:en:Kumārajīva|Kumārajīva]] between 402 and 412 CE.<ref name="HuntingtonBangdel2003"/> It contains the only mention of the [[Rig Veda]] in the entire [[:en:Chinese Buddhist canon|Chinese Buddhist canon]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Dalal |first1=Roshen |title=The Religions of India: A Concise Guide to Nine Major Faiths |date=2010 |publisher=Penguin Books India |isbn=9780143415176 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pNmfdAKFpkQC&q=Mahamayuri+Chinese+Buddhist+canon&pg=PA218 |language=en}}</ref>
Her origins are said to derive from an Indian goddess of the same name. She is one of the Nepali [[pañcarakṣā]] deities, where she is the chief of the five rakṣā (守護) in the five parasol configuration, occupying the northern position. Unlike her four companions of the pañcarakṣā she appears to have had a fairly well-developed cult in India.<ref name="myoo">{{cite web |title=JAANUS / Kujaku Myouou 孔雀明王 |url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/kujakumyouou.htm |website=www.aisf.or.jp}}</ref>
==Iconography== [[File:Pancaraksa, AD 1653, Ranjana script, Mahamayun Wellcome L0035405.jpg|thumb|left| A miniature depicting Mahamayuri from the [[Pañcarakṣā]]]] Despite being associated with the [[Wisdom King]]s, Mahāmāyūrī tends to be portrayed with a benevolent expression rather than a wrathful one.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.kyohaku.go.jp/eng/dictio/kaiga/kujaku.html|title=About the Buddhist Painting, Peacock Myoo (Mahamayuri)|work=Kyoto National Museum|access-date=17 November 2017|language=en}}</ref> She has three faces and six hands.<ref name=v>{{cite web |title=Buddhist Deity: Pancha Raksha Main Page |url=https://www.himalayanart.org/search/set.cfm?setID=736 |website=Himalayan Art Resources}}</ref>
According to the ''Ritual for Painting the Image and Setting Up the Altar of the Great Peacock King Buddha Mother'' (大孔雀明王畫像壇場儀軌), Mahamayuri is depicted as a white human-headed peacock with four arms. She is often seated on a lotus flower or a peacock throne. The right hand holds a lotus flower, which represents the purity of his wisdom. The second right hand holds the fruit of all causes, which represents his ability to fulfill all wishes. The left hand holds a wish-fulfilling fruit, which represents his ability to grant all desires. The second left hand holds three or five peacock tails, which represent his power to dispel poison and disease. In the Garbhadhātu Mandala, Mahamayuri is enshrined in the sixth position of the southernmost corner of the {{ill|Susiddhivīja|ja|蘇悉地院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susiddhivīja|qq=}}.<ref name="legend"/>
She is often portrayed riding a peacock and commonly sporting four arms. Although the items she holds vary among traditions, common items include a [[:en:Citron|Citron]], [[:en:bael fruit|bael fruit]], lotus flower, and a peacock tail feather. With the moon as a background, wear peaceful ornaments and garments. Seated in the half (vajrasana) posture.<ref name=v/>
==Legends== [[File:孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō.jpg|thumb|孔雀明王 - Kujaku Myōō Image of Mahāmāyūrī Vidyārājñī from the Taishō Shinshū Daizōkyō]] Legend holds that in primordial times, the original [[Phoenix (mythology)|Phoenix]] ([[:en:Fenghuang|Fenghuang]]), the leader of flying beings, gave birth to the peacock Mahamayuri and to the eagle named the [[:en:Dapeng Jinchi Mingwang|Golden-Winged Great Peng]]. The peacock once consumed the Buddha in one of his previous lives, who managed to escape by cutting through her stomach. At that time, the peacock preyed on humans, and the Buddha intended to kill it. However, the deities intervened and urged him to stop. In exchange for a promise to renounce its habit of preying on humans, the Buddha elevated the peacock to the status of his godmother, while the eagle became his uncle and was granted a high position in heaven.<ref>{{cite news |title=西遊最強姐弟，為何最後卻反目成仇，只因分別效忠兩位佛祖 |url=https://www.xuehua.us/2019/03/10/西游最强姐弟，为何最后却反目成仇，只因分别效/zh-tw/ |work=雪花新聞 |date=9 March 2019|language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊記中除了金翅大鵬，如來的親戚都是誰？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/CrkZgB |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=4 September 2017 |language=zh}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=9789866936333 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B+%E8%80%81%E9%B9%B0 |language=zh-TW}}</ref>
In the ''[[Mahamayuri Tantra]]'', it is stated that Mahamayuri assumes various forms to assist the Buddha, including taking on the human form of [[Queen Maya]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Mahamayuri Tantra |url=https://www.scribd.com/document/404209978/Mahamayuri-pdf |language=en}}</ref> [[:en:Kong Xuan|Kong Xuan]], a peacock spirit in the Ming Dynasty novel ''[[:en:Fengshen Yanyi|Fengshen Yanyi]]'', is based on the figure of Mahamayuri. Kong Xuan appears as a powerful general under [[:en:King Zhou of Shang|King Zhou of Shang]].<ref>{{cite book |title=中国精怪文化 [Chinese Ghost Culture] |date=1997 |publisher=上海人民出版社 |isbn=978-7-208-02454-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SSZhAAAAIAAJ&q=%E5%AD%94%E5%AE%A3+%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Journey to the West''=== The 16th-century Chinese novel ''[[:en:Journey to the West|Journey to the West]]'' includes a story about how the king of the Kingdom of Zhuzi had a hobby of [[:en:Hunting|Hunting]] when he was a young prince. One day, he accidentally killed two followers of Mahamayuri. Later on, Mahamayuri said that to pay for this, the prince would suffer a lovesickness for 3 years. At the time, the bodhisattva [[Kannon|Guanyin]]'s steed [[:en:Denglong (mythology)|Golden Haired Hou]] went across the scene and managed to hear this. Years later, when the prince had grown up and became the king of the kingdom, the Golden Haired Hou took advantage of the child who guards him sleeping, escaped to the mortal realm, became the demon king Sai Tai Sui, and kidnapped the Lady of Jinsheng Palace (金聖宮娘娘), one of the king's favorite consorts, and forced her to marry him. Without the lady, the king got sick for three years.<ref>{{cite news |title=赛太岁很听话？观音菩萨"打诳语"有水平_手机搜狐网 |url=https://www.sohu.com/a/107874164_109477/?pvid=000115_3w_a |work=Sohu|language=my|date=27 July 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=西遊朱紫國王射傷了孔雀明王的孩子，為何是觀音坐騎出面復仇？｜Zi 字媒體 |url=https://zi.media/@yidianzixun/post/Wjo7g5 |work=Zi 字媒體 |date=8 August 2017 |language=zh}}</ref>
===''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra''=== According to the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'', during the time of the Buddha, there was a recently ordained monk named Sāḍdhi. Despite his brief time as a monk, he had already received the precepts and was diligently studying the Vinaya rules. While engaged in gathering firewood and preparing a bath for fellow monks, Sāḍdhi encountered a perilous situation when a large black snake emerged from a hole in a decaying tree and bit him on his right big toe. The venom swiftly spread throughout his body, causing him to collapse unconscious on the ground, foaming at the mouth, with his eyes rolled back.<ref name="legend">{{cite book |title=孔雀明王行法: 摧伏毒害煩惱 |date=2008 |publisher=Buddhall Cultural / 全佛 |isbn=978-986-6936-33-3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eHyxmKm6a7UC&q=%E5%AD%94%E9%9B%80%E6%98%8E%E7%8E%8B |language=zh}}</ref>
[[:en:Ananda|Ananda]], witnessing the distressing scene, promptly sought the Buddha's assistance. In response, the Buddha informed Ananda about the potent mantra of the Mahamayuri (Great Peacock) Buddha Mother, emphasizing its ability to counteract poisons, dispel fears, avert calamities, and safeguard and nurture all sentient beings, leading them to happiness. The Buddha proceeded to impart the Mahamayuri Vidyarajni mantra to Ananda, who hastened back to Sāḍdhi and employed the mantra for healing. Through the power of the mantra, the poison was eradicated, and Sāḍdhi regained consciousness, fully restored to health. Expressing the significance of the mantra, the Buddha instructed Ananda to propagate it among all sentient beings, offering protection from harm and the opportunity to attain happiness. The ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' is a Mahayana Buddhist scripture that imparts the mantra of the Mahamayuri Buddha Mother. This mantra is reputed for its ability to safeguard against harm, alleviate ailments, and usher in happiness. Widely embraced by Mahayana Buddhism practitioners, the mantra is frequently chanted as a means of spiritual practice.<ref name="legend"/>
The Buddha imparted the ''Mahamayuri Vidyarajni Sutra'' with the specific intention of addressing the manifold challenges faced by sentient beings. According to the sutra, both the text and the mantra possess the extraordinary power to alleviate diseases, afflictions, and fears while serving as a protective shield for all living beings. Instructing Ananda to disseminate this sutra and mantra widely, the Buddha aimed to liberate sentient beings from suffering and guide them towards the attainment of happiness.<ref name="legend"/>
Delving into the origin of the Buddha's emphasis on this mantra, the sutra recounts a past life where the Buddha existed as a golden peacock king residing on the southern slope of [[:en:Mount Meru|Mount Meru]]. Engaging in the daily recitation of the mantra, the king experienced perpetual peace in mind and body. However, a lapse occurred when, driven by desire, he neglected the mantra to frolic with his peacock consorts in the forest. Succumbing to intoxication and a loss of mindfulness, he inadvertently fell into a hunter's trap. Bound and facing peril, he remembered the mantra, reciting it and miraculously securing his release, with his consorts emerging unscathed.<ref name="legend"/>
Recognizing the formidable efficacy of this mantra, the Buddha discerned its capacity to mitigate grave transgressions, eradicate minor wrongdoings, and dispel all forms of suffering. Out of compassion, the Buddha shared this transformative mantra with all sentient beings, envisioning that its constant recitation would empower individuals to transcend fear, surmount suffering, perpetually attract good fortune, evade danger, and live long and contented lives.<ref name="legend"/>
== In Chinese Buddhism == In China, she is known as Kǒngquè Míngwáng (孔雀明王) and her image is enshrined in many Chinese Buddhist temples. In contemporary times, her veneration is thought to be closely related to medical care, public health, environmental protection, and social welfare.
In [[:en:China|China]], some of the temples which venerate her include the [[:en:Luohan Temple (Chongqing)|Luohan Temple]] in [[:en:Chongqing|Chongqing]], [[Wuyou Temple]], [[:en:Baoguang Temple|Baoguang Temple]] and [[:en:Fuhu Temple|Fuhu Temple]] in [[Sichuan]], [[Shifo Temple]] in [[Shaanxi]], [http://www.jgms.net/ Jinguangming Temple] in [[:en:Fujian|Fujian]], as well as [[Qiongzhu Temple]] in [[Yunnan]]. In [[Taiwan]], her veneration is promoted by the [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about.html Chinese Great Peacock Buddha Association](中華世界大孔雀佛協會), which has their main headquarters located at [http://www.buda.org.tw/sd_about01.html Kaicheng Temple]. In [[Singapore]], one temple which venerates her is [[:en:Hua Giam Si|Hua Gim Si Temple]].
==In Japanese Buddhism== In Japan she was known as the name Kujaku Myōō (孔雀明王). There is a record stating that she was worshiped during the [[Nara period]], and her image was placed at the newly constructed [[Saidai-ji]] Temple Kondo (Saidai-ji Temple Golden Hall).<ref name="myoo"/>
== Gallery == <gallery> Banner with Bodhisattva, possibly Mahamayuri.jpg| Chinesischer Maler des 11. Jahrhunderts (II) 001.jpg| Dazu 2007 665.jpg| Mahamayuri statue.jpg| LK-colombo-gangaramaya-pfau.jpg| </gallery>
